title: The bedside salt bowl
subtitle: A folk-protection spell for the night that feels uneasy: a small bowl of salt, kept by the bed.
excerpt: A small folk-protection spell for the night that feels uneasy. A bowl of clean salt sits on the bedside table for one week, takes on what the night brings, and gets washed away on the seventh morning. Ten minutes to set up; one minute on each subsequent night.
sourceNotes: Salt as a folk-protection object appears across many cultural lineages, European hedge-witch traditions, Mediterranean evil-eye work, Japanese household practice, Appalachian folk magic, Hellenistic candle work. The seven-night bedside form used here is a general folk pattern documented across these traditions. Bedtime framing adapted from Day 12 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release fear of the night".

content.text: A small folk-protection spell for the night that feels uneasy. A bowl of clean salt sits on the bedside table for one week, holds whatever the night brings, and gets washed down the sink on the seventh morning. Ten minutes to set up; one minute on each subsequent night to refresh the intention.
New to spell work? Read
how spells work
first.
What you'll need
A small bowl, a tea cup, a ramekin, anything you can dedicate to this for a week.
Cooking salt, about two tablespoons, enough to fill the bowl two-thirds.
Optional: a candle and a match for the set-up evening.
The spell
Set up, the first evening
Sit on the bed, both feet on the floor. Pour the salt into the bowl. Hold the bowl in both hands for a slow minute. Light the candle if you are using one.
Say the intention, out loud or in your mind:

attrs.quote: This salt holds what the night brings. I rest with the night, and the night rests with me.

content.text: Set the bowl on the bedside table, where you'll see it on waking. Blow out the candle if you used one.
Each subsequent evening
Before getting into bed, sit beside the bowl for a minute. One hand resting next to it. Repeat the intention, slow, once. That's the whole of the nightly practice.
Close, the seventh morning
Take the bowl to the kitchen sink. Run cool water over the salt and let it dissolve completely. Rinse the bowl. The salt goes down the drain; the spell is closed.
If the week of nights helped, set up a fresh bowl. If it didn't, leave the bowl down and try a different practice.
When this isn't working
If the unease is more about a busy mind than the night itself, pair this with
the body scan for sleep
Where this practice comes from

Salt as a folk-protection object shows up across many cultural lineages. European hedge-witch traditions, Mediterranean evil-eye work, Japanese household practice (the small salt cones at the door of a shop), Appalachian folk magic, Hellenistic candle work. The seven-night bedside form used here is a general folk pattern. It draws on the candle-and-salt practice in hedge-witch craft. The bedtime framing is adapted from Day 12 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive by Rebecca J Page, 2025.
